item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: Re: Obvious(ly immature) question
item.content:encoded: because that's ew

item.title: Obvious(ly immature) question
item.content:encoded: How come nobody has tried putting semen in this thing yet?
This is a neat article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I realize this article has not been revisited in quite some time, but I am curious to know whether water from SCP-109 would have the same effect as ordinary water, or if its anomalous qualities would cause a differing result.

item.title: Re: Staff Post (Open)
item.content:encoded: Actually, given that this is an ongoing staff discussion, I feel that I need to step in and note that we are currently not giving permission for additions, given that this is one of the "Staff Protected" articles, due to its author's absence.

item.title: Staff Post (Open)
item.content:encoded:
Commenting upon request:

This piece does seem to be open to additions, based on the author's apparent intent with these two posts. With the caveat that since the author is absent and unable to dish out quality control, staff may revert (or tweak) any edits judged to be low quality.

See below. We'll update later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was gonna upvote but the rating is the same as the object number

lol not anymore apparently

also it's against the rules to vote according to something besides the article's merit

if you think a piece is good and you enjoy it just go upvote dude

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was gonna upvote but the rating is the same as the object number
i couldn't do it, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know if this has been suggested yet but why if you try liquid nitrogen or other condensed gasses?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this thing years back, and reading it again now that I'm a site member, I still love it. Shame the OP isn't around and it isn't open submissions - I'd love to toss a non-Newtonian fluid in there.

item.title: Re: I can Imagine
item.content:encoded: …I'm guessing you're joking, since we don't do "demoted to D-class" anymore.

item.title: I can Imagine
item.content:encoded: Dr.████████ was reported poring 30 ml of tequila into SCP-107 it resulted in it raining tequila for x hours and no affect was observed on the grass other than the standard water affect but fruit and vegetables developed to be alcoholic, Dr.████████ was immediately demoted to D-Class, and quoted saying "Worth it"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is cool. I wish I'd thought of something like that. +1 for well written novel idea.

item.title: Possible test?
item.content:encoded: I don't think anyone will see this, but I wonder if SCP-999 would work. I'm not entirely sure if it's a liquid, but it would be worth a shot. It's not like 999's dangerous. I'm also curious to see what would happen if we used 107 in space (not that it would be very safe). Oh, and can we throw some 107-produced water in 914? Might be fun.

item.title: great job
item.content:encoded: Very interesting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On that note, some form of desiccant should be placed in the container as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You both bring up an excellent point; I was making the big mistake of assuming everyone else is as intrigued by numbers as I am. Thanks for the quick reply.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That sounds like the kind of thing that would be addressed in supplementary documents that don't actually exist because none of us would want to read them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, this is where your approach goes wrong: A SCP wiki submission, while ostensibly a scientific document, doesn't have to be explicit or exact in order to be interesting. This is one of those cases where adding in a ton of numbers and formula would probably harm the narrative, thus I would prefer not to see this done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, it appears that the original author hasn't been around in a long while, and I don't just want to edit an article (especially considering that for intents and purposes I'm still a newbie).

However, it's rather obvious that there is a direct correlation between the volume of fluid placed in 107 and the duration of fluidfall:

Volume Inserted (mL) Duration of Fluidfall (hrs)
2 .13333…
10 .45
20 3.7
55 48

Shouldn't a course of action be taken so we can calculate exactly the milliliter to hour conversion? I've found two very accurate formulas from these four known inputs (assuming that the milliliters and hours are EXACT); all one would have to do next is use a different volume (say, 15 milliliters) and find which formula correctly outputs the duration of fluidfall:

Graph Type Formula (x is mL & y is hours) Variable a Variable b Variable c Variable d 15 mL equals:
Logistic y = c/(1 + ae^(-bx)) 874.0557615 .2141485966 48.32180357 1.335026314 hrs
Cubic y = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d .000095250574 .0128084631 -.1259292952 .3331960667 1.647631523 hrs

Now, obviously, this would mean that only those four previously-recorded volumes will for some reason give near EXACT minutes/hours, but it wouldn't be difficult to hand wave it by saying neither of these formulas are the perfect one, and 107 will use a formula but round to the nearest minute. I would like to add one of these formulas to the main page, but since I'm little more than a lurker at the moment I'm not going to do such without approval.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
whenever i see this

Result: A substance proven afterwards to be human blood with the same genetic makeup as the donating subject fell on the test site for 27 minutes.

i read

Result: , as the donating subject fell on the test site for 27 minutes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Putting this thing in its own room seems a bit excessive to me. You could very easily just vacuum seal it and store it in a safe somewhere and it would be just as effective. Seeing as how the testing site is in another location you would have to seal it to move it anyways. 5 meters square is an awfully large place to house something that is at most about 1.5 meters long but more likely about 0.6 meters long. I mean SCP-108 is a person and it only gets 3 m x 3 m.

Other than that I enjoyed this one. Lots of liquids to test it with. Most interested in knowing how it would handle a flammable substance. I just picture those fire plants from Mario everywhere. :P

item.title: Re: Raining Forever
item.content:encoded: Come to that, you could create eternal rain just by setting up a gutter running from the edge of the exclusion zone into the shell.

item.title: Re: Raining Forever
item.content:encoded: Well, maybe that, too. Meant to put ark.

item.title: Re: Raining Forever
item.content:encoded: …a story arc?

item.title: Raining Forever
item.content:encoded: What if someone were to "accidentally" leave 109 over 107? Would we need someone to start building an arc?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how the spit test made the plants spit back.

More tests we need to do sometime (that I can't come up with good results for):

  • Booze
  • Cooking oil
  • Flammable liquids
  • Coffee or soda
  • Sauces or other condiments
  • Mud
  • Soup or broth
  • Boiling water
  • Have a garden that fuels the site's cafeteria (use water or food liquids only)
  • Find uses for mutated plants (like plants from the blood test as a defense)
  • Run DNA tests on the affected plants

item.title: Re: Mentality
item.content:encoded: … wouldn't be a half-bad way of distributing amnesiacs on a mass scale… I mean, imagine if, say, 096 got out again. Would be a pretty good way to facilitate the cover-up once the damn thing gets re-contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm surprised nobody's sneezed in it yet.

item.title: Re: Mentality
item.content:encoded: So… Let's say that the shaman would've understood the concept of "liquid that makes you see really weird shit", AKA hallucinogens. Put liquid LSD in that cup, put in a zone where a recently-recovered SCP has been seen by a lot of people. Suddenly, everything can be dismissed as a really weird trip. Well, not really a serious suggestion, but the mere idea put a smile on lips.

item.title: Mentality
item.content:encoded:
The thing to understand about this is that its effects are based on the mindset of a paleolithic shaman.

It only works with liquids that such a shaman could have understood. That's why it rejected the mercury. It accepted the cyanoacrylate because a paleolithic shaman could understand the concept of "sticky stuff".

All the adulterated water tests get reduced to "ordinary water" because that's what the shell understands.

Elemental mercury is way outside the shaman's comprehension.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We are trying to move away from the Humorous Shenanigans with Terrible Punishments, or so I hear. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering the cut-ups the Foundation employs, I'm surprised nobody's made it rain martini yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, and cover it with saran wrap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shouldn't it be stored upsidedown?

Seems like it would reduce the risk of accidents.

item.title: Re: How about
item.content:encoded:
I didn't know about this when I added the line to the-things-dr-bright-is-not-allowed-to-do-at-the-foundation, but I just could see the horror show it would produce.

Too bad, because it's such a festive serving tray.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Funnily enough, I was beginning to think that when I added the last two tests. I don't think I had considered that the item would be tested with so many liquids (my fault), so only truly considered a few test cases. I'll think of a better, consistent result pattern and tweak the log accordingly. If you have any ideas, feel free to tell me.

EDIT: Log cleaned and modified for consistency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On rereading this, the effect feels a bit inconsistent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoops, thanks for pointing that out, I fixed the area (it's obvious once someone tells you!)

As for the tests, have a look, what do you think, feel free to add/edit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For that matter, what if you spit in it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eleven square kilometers is not enough space to contain an area of effect with a ten kilometer radius. I suspect you want Area-06 to be closer to a 22 km diameter circular area with the testing site at its center.

What would happen if natural liquids from other organisms were added to 107, such as fresh-squeezed orange juice, yak milk, or fish oil?

item.title: Re: How about
item.content:encoded: A test with 447-2 was in the original draft, but was recieved poorly (understandably, looking back). Raining 447-2 has a large chance of coming into contact with dead bodies. (I assume this is what you mean, 447-1 is just the ball that generates 447-2, at least I think so).

item.title: How about
item.content:encoded: … what if we try SCP-447-1?

item.title: Re: Containment
item.content:encoded: We need to justify our budget.

item.title: Containment
item.content:encoded: Why can't they just keep the thing in a safe or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, do vodka next.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Depends on what it's raining really, but yeah, your way does make more sense. Thinking about it it would probably take longer to get 120 to Site-19 than it would to get 107 out. I'll rewrite the containment procedures later, and post them here, see what people think.

EDIT: How about replacing the SCP-120 procedure with something along these lines:
"Should SCP-107 become active while within Site-19, two Class-D personnel are to remove it from the site via one of the item transport trucks in Loading Bay-02. This procedure must be followed regardless of the substance that activated SCP-107, given the unpredictable nature of the item's effect on plant matter. The item should only be returned once precipitation has ceased completely, and any abnormal plant growth has been neutralized."

This still makes for a relatively interesting procedure without being overly elaborate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems absurd to get the Puddlejumpers to lug the kiddie pool all the way to Site 19 from the mysterious whereabouts of Command-XX, sedate and murder the closest few prisoners, and toss the thing in when you could just put it in a truck and drive it into a testing area instead. I'm not even sure what threat making it rain in Site 19 poses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The containment procedures here are excellent. Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As the tap water was 'purified', sea water would simply result in pure water falling, and mercury would most likely result in mercury rain. What that would do to the plants is anyone's guess. If you have an interesting idea, please feel free to add to the reports!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about sea water and mercury?

item.title: Re: Stuff to test:
item.content:encoded:
I had not thought about self-sustainability (I wouldn't think so, perhaps the liquid flows into the base section, and there is a precipitation exclusion zone around the SCP?)

I assume you are referring to the part about SCP-120 when you describe containment as over-the-top. I suppose I was thinking about an incident where something like an output of SCP-294 was placed into the shell. What would you suggest in place of the (admittedly crazy) moon mission?

As for the tests, feel free to write up a test report =P

EDIT: I've redone the description to side-step the possibility of an infinite rain cascade.

item.title: Stuff to test:
item.content:encoded:
Something that is melted, like butter, wax or glass
Something that is not "melted" but solidifies rather fast, like glue
Colloids, like mayonnaise

Can de effect be interrupted by removing the liquid (i.e. using the sponge or upturning the SCP)?

Wouldn't the effect be somewhat self-sustaining if the object is left in the open? If so that should be noted accordingly. also some aspects of containment seem mildly over-the-top…
